digest.top_line: DeLLphi-304: CNS-PFS HR 0.54 (0.40 in brain-mets, 6.5 vs 4.2mo), tarlatamab delays intracranial progression in 2L SCLC (post-hoc).
digest.tldr: Thoracic led: DeLLphi-304's post-hoc intracranial signal (CNS-PFS HR 0.40 in brain-mets) reopens WBRT/PCI deferral at SCLC relapse, though most pts had prior cranial RT so it isn't shown to substitute. In rectal NOM, ctDNA caught only 41% of local regrowth vs 74% of distant mets, so it can't replace pelvic surveillance.

sites.intro: A DLL3 BiTE with intracranial activity, the open RT question is whether it defers WBRT/PCI at relapse.

studies.name: DeLLphi-304
studies.tldr: CNS-PFS HR 0.54 ITT, 0.40 in brain-met subgroup (6.5 vs 4.2mo); tarlatamab delays intracranial progression vs chemo in 2L SCLC.
details: 🔍 Post-hoc intracranial analysis of DeLLphi-304 (phase 3, 2L relapsed SCLC, tarlatamab vs chemo); CNS endpoints not the registered primary
🔍 Brain-met subgroup n=67 vs 56; >70% had prior CNS-directed treatment, so the signal sits on a pretreated intracranial background
📐 ITT CNS-PFS by RECIST/investigator (stratified Cox); brain-met subgroup by mRANO-BM/BICR (unstratified Cox)
📊 CNS complete-response duration favored tarlatamab: median NE (1.3+ to 13.6+) vs 3.6mo
⚠️ Short follow-up (median 11.4mo); durability of intracranial control beyond ~1yr unproven
⚠️ Post-hoc small-N subgroup with unstratified model; hypothesis-generating, not prespecified
🔗 RT read: a DLL3 BiTE with intracranial activity raises deferral of WBRT/PCI at relapse, but most pts here had prior cranial RT, so it is not shown to substitute for it

verdict.rationale: Explicitly post-hoc intracranial analysis; brain-met subgroup small (n=67 vs 56) with unstratified Cox, >70% pretreated for CNS. Design, not maturity, gates the read.
verdict.audience: Relapsed 2L SCLC with brain metastases (>70% prior CNS-directed treatment)

studies.significance: The intracranial signal (brain-met CNS-PFS HR 0.40, CR 14.9% vs 5.4%) lands on a pretreated background: >70% of the subgroup had prior CNS-directed therapy. That reframes the RT question from replacement to sequencing, whether a DLL3 BiTE with CNS activity supports deferring WBRT at relapse rather than substituting for it.
studies.monday_clinic: In relapsed SCLC with treated brain metastases entering 2L, this supports tarlatamab as a CNS-active systemic option; it does not extend to untreated brain metastases or replace upfront cranial RT.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Post-hoc** intracranial analysis of DeLLphi-304, a **phase 3 open-label RCT** of tarlatamab vs chemotherapy in 2L relapsed SCLC. ITT CNS endpoints by RECIST/investigator; brain-met subgroup by mRANO-BM/BICR.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: ITT **n=254 vs 255**. Brain-met subgroup **n=67 (tarlatamab) vs 56 (chemo)**; **>70% had received prior CNS-directed treatment**, so responses sit on a pretreated background.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: Time to CNS progression or death (CNS-PFS), CNS response, disease control, and duration. **No prespecified intracranial primary** (post-hoc).

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Intracranial efficacy favored tarlatamab across ITT and the brain-met subgroup on every CNS endpoint measured; magnitudes in the figure tables.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: The parent DeLLphi-304 randomized tarlatamab vs chemo in 2L SCLC; this analysis addresses intracranial disease, historically the domain of **WBRT/SRS ± PCI**. No head-to-head vs cranial RT.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents relapsed 2L SCLC with brain metastases, mostly previously treated for CNS disease. Does not represent untreated brain metastases or first-line SCLC.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Post-hoc, unplanned analysis; brain-met subgroup small with an **unstratified Cox model**. Median follow-up **~11.4 mo** limits the durability read. Open-label design.

open_questions: Durability of intracranial control beyond 1 year
Whether CNS activity permits deferring WBRT or PCI in 2L SCLC
Activity in untreated, RT-naive brain metastases

related_trials.brief_title: S1827 (MAVERICK) Testing Whether the Use of Brain Scans Alone Instead of Brain Scans Plus Preventive Brain Radiation Affects Lifespan in Patients With Small Cell Lung Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: This phase III trial studies magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) surveillance and prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) to see how well they work compared to MRI surveillance alone in treating patients with small cell lung cancer. MRI scans are used to monitor the possible spread of the cancer with an MRI machine over time. PCI is radiation therapy that is delivered to the brain in hopes of preventing spread of cancer into the brain. The use of brain MRI alone may reduce side effects of receiving…

Limited Stage Lung Small Cell Carcinoma

related_trials.brief_title: PRophylactic Cerebral Irradiation or Active MAgnetic Resonance Imaging Surveillance in Small-cell Lung Cancer Patients (PRIMALung Study)
related_trials.brief_summary: In this phase III study, the primary objective is to test with a one-sided significance of 5% whether for the treatment of small cell lung cancer (SCLC) patients, brain MRI surveillance alone is non-inferior in terms of overall survival compared to prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) combined with brain MRI surveillance in the entire study population.

sites.intro: ctDNA's low sensitivity for local regrowth keeps imaging plus clinical surveillance the backbone of nonoperative rectal management.

studies.name: ctDNA and Local Regrowth/Distant Mets in Nonoperative Rectal Cancer
studies.tldr: Sensitivity for local regrowth only 41% (vs 74% for distant mets); positive ctDNA is prognostic but can't replace NOM surveillance.

details.text: 📊 Per-sample diagnostic accuracy for events within ±90d of each draw (n=669 samples)

details: 🔍 INTERCEPT cohort, N=110 MSS rectal adenoca; cCR/nCR after NAT then NOM; serial tumor-informed ctDNA (Signatera Exome)
🔍 Baseline cT3 72/110, cN1-2 60/110; NAT was TNT in 104/110; median f/u 25 mo
📊 Events on NOM: local regrowth 23 (21%), distant mets 12 (11%)
📊 Salvage after ctDNA+ regrowth had more ypT3-4 (75% [6/8] vs 21% [3/14], p=0.01)
⚠️ 41% sensitivity for regrowth: a negative ctDNA can't clear the pelvis, imaging + clinical surveillance stays the backbone
⚠️ First post-NAT ctDNA claim rests on only 12 evaluable pts (Fig 2); single-institution cohort
🔗 ctDNA tracks distant relapse better than local regrowth, consistent with low shedding of localized luminal rectal disease

figures.caption: KM by ctDNA status. Log-rank p=0.0002 to <.0001 (Fig 1 longitudinal; Fig 2 first post-NAT within 180d).

verdict.rationale: Single-institution prognostic cohort (N=110), only 12 evaluable early draws; 41% sensitivity can't support a surveillance threshold. Hypothesis-generating adjunct, needs prospective validation.
verdict.audience: MSS rectal adenoca, cCR/nCR after neoadjuvant therapy, on watch-and-wait

studies.significance: In watch-and-wait after TNT, ctDNA can't replace pelvic surveillance: sensitivity for local regrowth is only 41% (12/29), so a negative result doesn't clear the pelvis. It reads better as a distant-relapse and adverse-biology flag (mets sensitivity 74%; ctDNA+ at regrowth carried more ypT3-4, 75% vs 21%).
studies.monday_clinic: Applies to MSS rectal cancer in nonoperative management after cCR/nCR to neoadjuvant therapy: a negative ctDNA does not support easing imaging surveillance, and a positive one flags distant-relapse risk; it does not extend to upfront-surgery candidates or MSI-high disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: INTERCEPT program cohort, **N=110** MSS rectal adenocarcinoma (2020-2024), all with cCR or nCR to neoadjuvant therapy then nonoperative management. Serial tumor-informed ctDNA (Signatera Exome); median follow-up **25 mo**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Baseline cT3 in 72/110, cT4 in 11; cN1-2 in 60/110. Neoadjuvant was **TNT in 104/110** (CRT 5, chemo 1). Initial response cCR 69, nCR 41; median age 56.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: Prognostic association of ctDNA (longitudinal and first post-NAT within 180d) with local regrowth and distant metastasis; per-sample diagnostic accuracy for events within ±90d of each draw.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Local regrowth **23 (21%)**, distant mets **12 (11%)**. Ever-positive ctDNA associated with worse 2-year regrowth-free and metastasis-free survival; per-sample accuracy in the table.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents MSS rectal adenocarcinoma in nonoperative management after cCR/nCR to neoadjuvant therapy (mostly TNT). Does not represent upfront-surgery candidates, MSI-high tumors, or non-responders taken to TME.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Single-institution cohort with only **12 evaluable** first post-NAT draws and 29 regrowth-associated samples. **41%** per-sample sensitivity means a negative ctDNA cannot exclude local regrowth; not a validated surveillance threshold.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Signal is prognostic, not a surveillance replacement: ctDNA flags distant relapse and adverse biology better than in-pelvis regrowth (ctDNA+ at regrowth carried more **ypT3-4, 75% vs 21%**, p=0.01), consistent with low shedding of localized luminal rectal disease.

open_questions: Optimal integration of ctDNA with endoscopy/MRI in NOM surveillance
Does ctDNA-guided surveillance change salvage or survival outcomes?

related_trials.brief_title: ctDNA-Informed Management of Early-Stage Rectal Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: This is a phase 2 pragmatic study to examine the utility of ctDNA-informed treatment management for participants with early-stage rectal cancer using the Signatera Genome assay. The primary aims are to 1) assess pathologic complete response (path CR) in the ctDNA informed management arm; 2) assess pathologic complete response (path CR) in the post total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT) standard of care (SOC) surgery arm; and 3) assess disease free survival (DFS) in the ctDNA informed management arm.

interventions.name: Signatera Genome ultra-sensitive ctDNA blood test + total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT)

related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Tumor tissue histologically confirming rectal adenocarcinoma that is available for Natera ctDNA assay.
* Cohort A only: Patients appropriate for receiving TNT including chemotherapy and chemoradiation.
* Cohort B only: TNT must have included at least 4 cycles of CAPEOX or 6…
related_trials.answers_question: Does ctDNA-guided surveillance change salvage or survival outcomes?

related_trials.brief_title: ctDNA-guided Surveillance for Stage III CRC, a Randomized Intervention Trial
related_trials.brief_summary: IMPROVE-IT2 is a randomized multicenter trial comparing the outcomes of ctDNA guided post-operative surveillance and standard-of-care CT-scan surveillance. The hypothesis of this study is that ctDNA guided post-operative surveillance combining ctDNA and radiological assessments could result in earlier detection of recurrent disease and identify more patients eligible for curative treatment.
related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Colon or rectal cancer, tumor stage III (pT1-4N1-2,cM0) or stage II high-risk (pT4N0,cM0 and pT3N0, cM0 with risk factors)
* Have received curative intend resection and be candidates for adjuvant chemotherapy

Exclusion Criteria:

* Not treated with adjuvant chemotherapy

image_ocr_texts:
Association Between Circulating Tumor DNA and Local Tumor Regrowth and Distant Metastasis
During Nonoperative Management in Stage H-III Rectal Cancer
Kentaro Ochiai, Megan E. Delisle, Alisha H Bent, Van K. Morris, Arvind Dasari, Emma B. Holliday, Timothy E Newhook, Kristin Alfaro, Kathryn Aziz, Paula Marincola Smith, Michael G. White, Y. Nancy You,
Brian K. Bednarski, Ramy S. Behman, Jeongyoon Moon, Craig A. Messick, George J. Chang, J. Joshua Smith, Scott Kopetz, Tsuyoshi Konishi
The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, TX
The INTERCEPT Program
THE UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS
MD Anderson
Cancer Center
Making Cancer Hatory
Background
• During nonoperative management (NOM) for rectal cancer after a clinical
complete response (cCR) or near-cCR (nCR), 25-30% develop local
regrowth and 5-10% develop distant metastasis, highlighting the needs of
improved risk stratification and surveillance.
• Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) has emerged as a prognostic biomarker.
but its utility in NOM decision making remains undefined.
Methods
• In the INTERCEPT program, we studied 110 patients (2020-2024) with;
• Stages I-Ili, microsatellite stable rectal adenocarcinoma
> cCR / nCR after neoadjuvant therapy (NAT) and underwent NOM
• Surveillance with tumor-informed ctDNA testing (Signatera™ Exome)
• Outcomes:
1) Local regrowth and/or distant metastasis by longitudinal cIDNA status
2) Local regrowth and/or distant metastasis by the first post-treatment
CIDNA status (within 180 days post NAT)
3) Per-sample accuracy for local regrowth and/or distant metastasis
occurring within +90 days of each blood draw
4) Pathological characteristic of salvage surgery for local regrowth by
CIDNA status
Results
Table 1. Patient characteristics
Characteristics
Conclusions
• Positive ctDNA predicts local regrowth and distant recurrence after NOM for rectal cancer
• Sensitivity of ctDNA for detecting local regrowth is limited
ctDNA should be integrated as an adjunct tool with imaging and clinical assessments
Results
1) Patients with ever positive longitudinal ctDNA had an associated worse 2-year local regrowth-free and metastasis-free survival than those with persistentiy negative ctDNA (Figure 1)|
2)
Among patients with an evaluable first post-treatment ctDNA (n=12), those with a positive result had an associated lower regrowth-free and metastasis-free survival (Figure 2)
In per-sample analysis (n=669), ctDNA showed low sensitivity and high specificity for local regrowth and higher sensitivity and specificity for distant metastasis (Table 2)
4) Twenty-two of 23 patients with local regrowth underwent salvage surgery; cIDNA positivity at local regrowth was associated with more advanced pathological T stage (Table 3)

bookmarks.text: Dr. @g_mountzios #ASCO26 presents CNS outcomes with 2L tarlatamab in DeLLphi-304. Improved time to CNS progression overall (HR 0.54). In pts with brain nets, tarlatamab vs chemo CNS CR rate 15% vs 5% with DCR 78% vs 71% and time to CBS progression 6.5m vs 4.2m, HR 0.40 pic.twitter.com/5i8jL1zlKW
bookmarks.html:
Dr. @g_mountzios #ASCO26 presents CNS outcomes with 2L tarlatamab in DeLLphi-304. Improved time to CNS progression overall (HR 0.54). In pts with brain nets, tarlatamab vs chemo CNS CR rate 15% vs 5% with DCR 78% vs 71% and time to CBS progression 6.5m vs 4.2m, HR 0.40 pic.twitter.com/5i8jL1zlKW

— Stephen V Liu, MD (@StephenVLiu) June 1, 2026

Tarlatamab was associated with a better intracranial complete response rate, duration of intracranial complete response,
and duration of intracranial disease control compared to chemotherapy.
At the data-cutoff date of January 29, 2025, the median study follow-up was 11.4 months (tarlatarnab) and 11.5 months (chemotherapy). + denotes censored data.
*As most patients (>70%) had received prior treatment for CNS disease, specified outcomes were CR, non-CR/non-PD, and PD.
*Assessed in patients with 2 1 lesion that was 2 10mm in diameter.
Cl, confidence interval; CNS, central nervous system; CR, complete response; N1, number of patients with CNS lesions ≥ 10mm in diameter; NE, not estimable; PD, progressive disease.
